item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it was taken by the New York Botanical Gardens (as indicated here), with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i upvoted this because "if a tree falls in the woods and no one is around to hear it does it make a noise" but then it was more serious and remains upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this person belongs in an insane asylum, not as an SCP. Interesting concept though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not seeing it from my PC either

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last edit made it so I can't see the letter at the end on my phone. Is it just broken on mobiles or is it deleted completely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a tree falls in the forest, and no one is around, does it still make a sound?

Yes. Yes it does. Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels… contrived. I like the base anomaly, but the backstory doesn't bring anything new to the table, in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the signification of the name considering the story of the girlfriend, but it's true it does not sound like the kind of detail that Foundation people would put in a footnote. Maybe I'll incorporate that detail in the letter instead, if I re-write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, Virginia, someone does hear them.

I like it, though I think the footnote is completely superfluous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with both Kalinin and Roget. I'd upvote this if you cleaned up the tone. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I have new idea bubbling in my head and I will surely update it when they come more into focus. I have an idea this abnormality could be a self-imposed penance for all the trees he cut in his work, in the hope that the Forest will reveal to him the secret of what happened to his girlfriend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. I smiled a bit when I figured out that we had solved the "tree falls and no one's around" thing. Overall, it wasn't terribly gripping but it was interesting enough to merit an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think there's something good here, but you need to twist it a bit more. I like the idea of someone becoming so obsessed with counting the countless because of the loss of a loved one, but it feels too straightforward. I think this is one of those ideas that will really be stunning if you stew on it awhile more and take it a step beyond keeping track of trees that fall in the forest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry I did not see your comments before!

When I first had this idea I wasn’t sure what to do with it. I was wondering about making it a funny piece, or maybe a –J. I knew it could not work just on itself and it needed some kind of story, so I decided to go for something more dramatic.

I decided to make a parallel with the high number of murdered or disappeared aboriginal woman in the more remote locations. If someone gets murdered and nobody else cares, it's like a tree falling and not making a sound. So the guy is convinced his girlfriend has been murdered and is tormented by not knowing what happened exactly, and by how little people care.

The letter is trying to hint that the anomalous properties developed following something the guy did. Possibly communicate with the trees of the forest to find out what happened to his girlfriend (guided by a friend who has a ''special'' knowledge of plants). His present condition could be due to an adverse side-effect, or possibly some form of price he paid for the knowledge. Even he doesn’t know anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel indifferent about this no vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While agreeing with Kalinin's critique, I didn't feel emotionally manipulated by feels and enjoyed the note. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is in need of a thorough proofreading. Between that, the "lol if a tree falls in the forest and no one's around" thing, and the all-too-predictable visit from the Feels SledgehammerTM at the end, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit Cuddles I just posted a review on your draft
Like I said before, I thought that this was ok. The concept itself is mediocre, but the note keeps it from a downvote.
